He heard two men talking in the dark of the coal mine. They were talking about a school — a real school — where poor students could work their way through. That was all he needed to hear. Booker T. Washington had no money, no map, and no plan. He just started walking. Five hundred miles. Hungry, cold, and refused at hotels because of his skin. But he did not stop. When he finally arrived, dirty and exhausted, they handed him a broom. He swept that room like his whole life depended on it. It did.
